tspan.tspan: This document should
not
be used as a prescriptive guide
for
how sitelen pona characters ought to look like.
This document aims to provide a guide to those who want to
design sitelen pona glyphs that can be easily recognized by
the sitelen pona user community as it stands today. To that
end, this document analyzes:
2 geometric fonts
(nasin-nanpa by jan Itan and linja laso by
mute ante);
4 fonts closer to handwritten sitelen pona
(linja lipamanka
by lipamanka, sitelen seli kiwen by Rebecca Bettencourt,
nishiki-teki-lili and nasin sitelen pu, both by jan Ke Tami, the
former reduced from nishiki-teki by ufmihotaru, the latter
recreates the glyphs from the original pu, still relevant to
this day).
This document will not document characters for obscure
words or obscure alternate characters; only those discussed
in the 3-tiered Common Sitelen Pona standard.
We start with basic sitelen pona anatomy and radicals.
Characters with “heads” (mi, sina, ona, lape) are discussed
first for their importance (mostly pronouns) and similarity.
Then comes the word characters (ordered alphabetically),
then the punctuation characters (ordered by usage tier).
If in doubt about glyph design, contact the association for
advice!

text.tspan: other than the fonts mentioned in the "About this document"
section, this document uses Jost* by indestructible type*,
Bricolage Grotesque by Mathieu Triay and Fairfax HD by
Rebecca Bettencourt. this document was made by mute ante
© 2026 sitelen pona publishers and typographers association
mi, sina, ona, lape (characters with heads)
sitelen pona is a logographic writing system
for toki pona, so each character here indica-
tes a separate word. sitelen pona's glyphs
have high iconicity, meaning they represent
their meaning visually in some way most of
the time. the relative proportions, width,
height and size of glyphs can vary between
they are also mostly composed of recurring
simple shapes called radicals, like circles,
lines, rectangles and curves. they can appear
as different propotions and sizes in different
glyphs. dots are usually of the same size,
whether they're used for the position glyphs
or as creatures' eyes. circles can be substitu-
glyphs and fonts (l. lipamanka and n. s. pu
have some glyphs be smaller), though most
-nanpa) are monospaced, so they all fit within
the same em-box. they are centered relative
to it, with some optical adjustments and
ted for ovals to take up more character
width. a radical to look out for are emmitters,
like those shown below. their lengths and
point of origin can also differ between fonts
and glyphs to better fit their shapes.
The approximate center of the emitters will
be represented by an orange circle.
mi, sina and ona are the 1st, 2nd and 3rd
means “sleep/rest”. All 4 are a smaller circle
with a line appended. The pronouns's lines
are tangent to the circle, while lape's juts out
mi's circle is above, the line tangent to its left
side. sina's circle sits below, the line also
tangent to its left side. ona's circle sits to the
left, the line tangent to its bottom side.
lape's circle also sits to the left, but the line
comes off its leftmost side, horizontally.
the line can be curved or straight.
a single-story/cursive letter ɑ with a single leg towards the
right, curved or straight, with a line above it, vertical or
slightly angled towards the right. It can be confused for “o”.
a tall oval shape cut by 2 or 3 horizontal lines (2 is preferred
by the Common Sitelen Pona standard), with 2 dots above to
represent eyes. they can be further or closer to the main
the circle should not be more than 1/2 the
em height, so they can't be confused for
each other (see early font linja pona below).
an X. its dimensions can be taller than wide, or a square, with
a semicircle/circle segment with its flat end to the left, and an arrow passing
through it pointing to the right, resembling a bow and arrow.
an infinity symbol/turned eight, a bit wider than the usual character width.
a shallow box with a dot under it.
2 angled lines on top of each other, their points towards the middle.
a tall oval shape cut by 2 or 3 horizontal lines (2 is preferred
by the Common Sitelen Pona standard), with 2 dots above to
represent eyes. they can be further or closer to the main
an X. its dimensions can be taller than wide, or a square, with
a semicircle/circle segment with its flat end to the left, and an arrow passing
through it pointing to the right, resembling a bow and arrow.
an infinity symbol/turned eight, a bit wider than the usual character width.
a shallow box with a dot under it.
